final_answer:
No, it is still not fully like BSC validator additions.

Why this matters: validator-add is safer than before, but parity is still partial, so validator onboarding/election behavior can still diverge from BSC in production.

Confirmed read-only findings on what was just implemented:
1. Validator join is now native-path execution and ignores bridge-event metadata in logic: [msg_server.go](/srv/repos/gold-chain/bridge/gilt-consensus/x/stake/keeper/msg_server.go:37).
2. Bridge-driven validator-join broadcast is now skipped: [stake.go](/srv/repos/gold-chain/bridge/gilt-consensus/bridge/processor/stake.go:97).
3. `MsgValidatorJoin` schema still carries legacy event fields (`tx_hash/log_index/block_number/nonce`) for compatibility: [tx.proto](/srv/repos/gold-chain/bridge/gilt-consensus/proto/giltconsensusv2/stake/tx.proto:31).
4. Validator-set update logic is still epoch/activity based, not BSC-style stake-ranked candidate/cabinet election: [validator_set.go](/srv/repos/gold-chain/bridge/gilt-consensus/x/stake/types/validator_set.go:617).

What should be done next:
1. Hard-cut `MsgValidatorJoin` to pure native create-validator schema (remove legacy event fields, not just ignore them).
2. Implement BSC-equivalent validator selection/election policy (ranked candidate set and active-set rules).
3. Add parity tests for validator creation + election transitions, then rerun full `bridge/gilt-consensus` test suite before calling it complete.
4. Read-only mode respected here: no code changes in this reply.
